title: Valid Non-Attacking Knight Placement with Constrained Moves

statement.markdown: Given a chessboard of size n x n and a list of moves, determine if it's possible to place n knights such that none of them attack each other. However, there's a constraint: each knight can only move to the center square of a rank or file or to the center square of a diagonal. The knights are distinguishable, and their moves are independent of each other.
statement.inputFormat: The input format consists of an integer n followed by a list of n knights, where each knight is represented as a pair of coordinates (i, j) in the range [0, n-1]. The moves are represented as a list of pairs (x, y) indicating the possible moves from a center square.
statement.outputFormat: Return true if it's possible to place the knights without attacking each other, false otherwise.
statement.notes: The center squares of a rank or file are the middle squares of each row and column (for an 8x8 board, these are (3,0), (3,1), (3,2), (3,3), (3,4), (3,5), (3,6), (3,7) and similarly for columns). The center squares of a diagonal are the squares (1,1), (1,7), (6,1), (6,7) for an 8x8 board, and so on.

examples.explanation: Place the knights on the center squares without attacking each other.

hints.text: Consider using a recursive backtracking algorithm to find a valid placement of knights.

editorial.title: Optimal Solution
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved using a recursive backtracking algorithm. The key is to choose the next knight and its position carefully, considering the available and occupied positions on the board.
editorial.bruteForce: This problem can be solved using a brute force approach by generating all possible placements of the knights and checking if they are valid.
editorial.optimalSolution: We will use a recursive backtracking algorithm to find a valid placement of knights.
commonMistakes: not considering the constraints of the moves
